goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena!

You said you're feeling at a 72 this morning with high energy mode, which is interesting because yesterday your energy and focus scores were critically low — 42 and 37 respectively — while your mood stayed high at 85. That's a pretty big gap to carry into a new day.

Let's start with last night: you got 5.05 hours of sleep, which is a bit short for you, but your sleep efficiency was excellent at 95% and your HRV was very strong at 141ms. That means your body recovered well despite the shorter duration. The real story, though, is the timing yesterday: you had caffeine at 10:58 PM and your last meal was also at 10:58 PM, which is very late. Even with good sleep efficiency, that late stimulant and food timing might be contributing to the disconnect between how your body feels (strong recovery) and how you feel (exhausted).

Your activity yesterday was solid — 101 active minutes and nearly 12,000 steps — but your subjective energy was still in the basement. That mismatch suggests your perception of energy isn't keeping up with your physical capacity, which is why today's focus is on aligning those two things.

Today is about protecting your energy by managing timing cues and giving your perception a chance to catch up with your physiology.

You've got the physical foundation — let's help your mind feel it too.

assumptions: User can adjust evening timing for sleep despite tight schedule
Nutrition confidence (0.46) suggests estimates may be inaccurate
Journal missingness limits emotional/stress context
6am basketball practices may impact early wake times
soft_constraints: Leverage high energy for sleep routine implementation
Caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits
Consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend (sedentary minutes 584, slope -28.47)

generated_actions.title: Set a 2:00 PM caffeine cutoff alarm today
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday you had 128mg caffeine from a cappuccino at 22:58 PM, contributing to a very late stimulant intake despite excellent sleep efficiency (94.98%). Evening caffeine coincides with the 'late_stimulants_with_early_sleep_onset' cross-domain signal and can disrupt sleep onset and next-day energy. This external cue directly addresses the 'nutrition_timing' status and supports your sleep goal and today's 'sleep_consistency_recovery' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did your 2:00 PM alarm help you stop caffeine intake on time?
evaluation.success_definition: No caffeine is logged after 2:00 PM today AND user reports the alarm was helpful

assumptions: You have a phone or alarm device
generated_actions.description: Set a recurring alarm for 2:00 PM on your phone. When it rings, that's your signal to have your last caffeine of the day.

generated_actions.title: Plan an earlier dinner today, aiming to finish by 7:30 PM
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's last meal was at 22:58 PM (1378 minutes, very late) and the eating window was 898 minutes (~15 hours), trending sharply later (+83.32 slope). This directly contradicts the soft constraint 'maintain earlier eating window (<1200 min)' and the 'nutrition_timing' below_avg status. Proactively planning an earlier dinner today addresses the 'late_stimulants_with_early_sleep_onset' cross-domain signal and supports sleep recovery.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you successfully finish dinner by 7:30 PM?
evaluation.success_definition: Last meal occurs before 7:30 PM (time_last_meal_minutes < 1050) AND user reports intentional planning helped

assumptions: You can adjust your evening schedule
generated_actions.description: Given your tight schedule, proactively plan your dinner timing. Aim to have your final meal (like the chicken aioli wrap) completed by 7:30 PM local time.

generated_actions.title: When you finish a major work block, take a 4-minute standing and breathing reset
generated_actions.rationale: Despite substantial activity yesterday (101 MVPA minutes), your focus was critically low (37) and declining sharply (-9.23 slope), while workload perception is rising (+7.24 slope) and sedentary time is declining (-28.47 slope). This tiny break directly addresses the 'high_activity_with_low_energy_perception' cross-domain signal and the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' constraints.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take at least one 4-minute standing/breathing break after a work block today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement and breathing break after prolonged focused work

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
You have a timer or clock
generated_actions.description: After you complete a significant task or 60-90 minutes of focused work, stand up immediately. Stretch your arms overhead, take 4 deep breaths, and look away from your screen for 4 full minutes.

generated_actions.title: After brushing teeth tonight, write one sentence about today's best moment
generated_actions.rationale: Journal data is missing (confidence 0.9 but no content), limiting understanding of contextual factors behind the severe subjective-objective mismatch (energy 42, focus 37 vs high HRV 141). Yesterday's emotions were 'Tired' and 'Happy' — a mixed affective state. Habit-stacking a positive micro-reflection to your bedtime routine addresses the 'follow-up on journal missingness' soft constraint, bridges the perception gap, and ends the day on a reinforcing note to support sleep and stability.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you write one positive sentence after brushing teeth?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the written reflection

assumptions: You have pen and paper or a note app
generated_actions.description: Right after you finish brushing your teeth before bed, take 1 minute to write one positive sentence in a notebook about the best part of your day.

must_avoid: overthinking — keep it to one sentence
requires_equipment: pen and paper or digital note app

previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sleep was fragmented (wake ratio 14.53%, rising +1.96 slope) and efficiency was declining (-1.95 slope) despite a very late last meal (1331 min/~10:11 PM). This habit-stack directly addresses the cross-domain signal 'late_eating_with_sleep_fragmentation' and the 'sleep_debt' risk flag by creating a clear boundary between eating and sleep preparation to improve sleep continuity.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Add a protein source to your lunch salad
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your protein intake was adequate yesterday (108.7g) and trending upward (+4.40 slope), but your lunch was 'chicken pilaf rice and side salad' – reinforcing this pattern by explicitly adding protein to the salad addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and the soft constraint 'caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits' to support recovery.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Hey Ameena — hope you're having a great morning.

You rated your energy a **93** this morning, which is seriously high — you’re feeling strong and ready. Last night’s sleep, though, tells a different story: you got 5.9 hours and woke up a lot (14.5% of the night), so your body didn’t get the deep, continuous rest it’s used to. The weird part is that your HRV was excellent at 116.5 — your nervous system is still showing resilience — but that’s been happening for four days now while sleep keeps slipping. There’s a clear pattern here: you ate late last night (around 10:11 PM) and had an iced latte in the evening, and that lines up directly with the fragmented sleep. Your energy is sky-high (95), but your focus crashed to 40 — that’s a 55‑point gap, which usually means your brain is running on fumes even if your mood says otherwise.

The bright spot is your activity — you crushed 14k steps and 63 minutes of solid movement yesterday, and your stress stayed low. That’s a real strength to lean into.

Today is about protecting your sleep tonight and giving your focus a chance to catch up with your energy. The actions focus on simple boundaries: cutting caffeine earlier and creating a clean break between dinner and bed.

You’ve got the momentum from yesterday’s movement — today’s about channeling that into a calmer, more focused evening.

evidence_quotes: I love going to the gym in the morning, it makes me feel better all day
I had a big nap and I felt so much better afterwards

meal_descriptions.description: 2 egg and cheese muffins, a hashbrown and some sliced carrots, cucumber, green olives with a spoon of hummus.
a beef burger with a side of fries
